speaker: interviewer
text: --gang Janek Sendel. Let's go back now to-- back to Sasow and when the Germans were there. You mentioned earlier that you-- at times you could buy yourself out of-- do you want to explain?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. When the Germans came in, it wasn't very long, they-- they took all the young people to the labor camps. And those that had money could buy themselves out after two, three weeks. And then a few weeks later, they did the same thing. They did it a few times. I remember we lived like-- like next to, you know, to the Sasow rabbi. He was very, very well known all-- all over Europe. I think he had three sons. And I remembered the Germans chasing them. And they-- and they could run faster than the Germans, but they used to catch him. And a few times he bought himself out and then that was it. And then, when they went to the ghetto, only the women and children went to the ghetto. Most of the young men were in the labor camps already.

speaker: interviewer
text: The house that you lived in in Sasow, did it have electricity?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. We had no electricity, no water. We had-- we had a pump in the middle of the town. We had wells. And we had kerosene lamps and wood and coal.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's how you heated the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's how we heated the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's how your-- your-- your mother did the cooking?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But we didn't know any better and it was OK. See, today, we wouldn't want to go back to that, but we had no problems. As a kid, I mean, I-- I had everything that I needed, that I wanted. My parents, probably, you know, they-- they worked hard. They struggled, but the kids didn't know from these things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back now, or let's go forward. You-- you were in with the partisans. You were--

speaker: interviewee
text: After-- after the partisans, when we met with the Russians, it was right near Chelyabinsk, in the woods. Then, you know, they questioned us and everything. Everything was OK, and they took us. Me, they didn't want to take, but the others vouched for me that I helped them, so they took me.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were about 50 of you, you say.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there were about 15, 20. Then from Chelyabinsk, we-- we marched to Dubna, was about 40 kilometers, from 4:00 in the morning till 6:00 at night through a very big snowstorm. When we came there, you know, like, there was like a headquarters, whatever, barracks. We stayed there I think a couple of days. And, you know, they asked everybody their name, age, and this and that. So anyway, I made myself like two years older. And instead of the Russian Army with them, they send me into the Polish Army, because-- because it was Poland before the war. In 1939, it was Poland. So they sent me into the Polish Army. And they sent us to Sumy. It's in Russia. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get to Sumy?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train. Cattle wagons. It took-- it took us a long time to get to Sumy. When we-- when we crossed Kiev, on the other side of Kiev, was the station was Darnytsia. I remember Dnieper, the Germans bombed us right where we were in the middle. And as soon as we crossed the Dnieper River, we jumped from the train while the train was still moving. And they used to throw those night balloon lights. You could find a-- a needle in a haystack.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they bombed us there, going there. Anyway, we got to Sumy after. It took a couple days to-- to-- to fix the rails on the station. I think the name was Darnytsia. It was still winter?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was-- I think it was March or so. It was like-- it wasn't-- it wasn't winter. It was like spring or something like that. The snow was melting already. Then, when we came to Sumy, we were stationed on top of a mountain, like below the Russian village, and there, they-- they trained us. They gave us uniforms and trained us. We didn't have no guns, but they gave us like sticks, brooms. And we-- we-- we got through it in a couple of months of very hard training there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was training you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Polish Army, the officers. And then from Sumy, they send us down to Kiwerce. Kiwerce is near Lutsk, already, in Poland, Lutsk. And we came down to Kiwerce in uniform and everything. And they, like, they separate us to each, you know, the company here, you know, the division, company, battalion. Me, the commander was General Laskin and Colonel Sadovski. He was from [POLISH]. When I came down there in uniform, he took me in with him. I was with him for about two months. He had a adjutant with his own jeep, a chauffeur. His name was Nikoli Kolya. Again, the other-- the general, he had also his own jeep and a adjutant, and his name was Janek. He was Polish. They were both Polish. And in Kiwerce, they stayed in the village. They each had a house. It was guarded. They had-- they had female guard there, there was a whole company of Polish, you know, like girls in the army. And they-- they were guarding like you know, gasoline, and-- and ammunition dumps, and the headquarters. And I was with that colonel for about two months. Like, with him, stayed with him. I used to go with him to the meetings, like the, you know, the war meetings.

speaker: interviewer
text: You also had a-- a-- a rifle or a gun?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. I didn't have nothing at the time, nothing. And then I remember what's his name, Rekosovski. Marshall Rekosovski was-- he was-- he was the commander of the-- of the first Polish-Russian Army east of the front. Like, he asked me if I wanted to go to Moscow. It's called the [SPEAKING RUSSIAN]. It's a military school, because they only take Russian, I mean, the officer's kids. I didn't want to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, I stayed in for two months, then they recalled him, that Russian, Polkovnik. He was wearing a Polish uniform, but he was a colonel. They were commanders of the First Army, the Polish Army. And the name was Domborskego. OK? And I was in the second division. So when they took they-- they recalled the colonel to Zhytomyr, I went with him. Like we took him back to Zhytomyr, Russia, me and the driver. They took him there and then we went back.

speaker: interviewee
text: But-- but he gave orders, like in the headquarters, they should take me in, like, to the communications. And I went through this communications school, you know, Morse code, radio, all that. And I was there till the end. And they-- they showed us rifles and everything after. Like when they gave everybody, they-- they put them like in two different divisions, you know, in camp. So everybody would get a rifle. I got a rifle too, a short one. But the Russian rifles, they had the long ones and the short. The cavalry used to use the little, the short ones. The long one was too tall for me, so I had that rifle till-- till I got out of the Army. I was responsible for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had both a rifle and a handgun?

speaker: interviewee
text: The handgun I wasn't issued, only officers. But every soldier from the front line had a revolver. I took-- I took that revolver from a German officer. And I had it till the end of the war. I left it in Poland. Anyway, from when-- after when I got into the communications, I went through a school and everything and then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Approximately how long were you trained in communications?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say about three months to three months. Like it was-- I was trained on the job. It wasn't like a school. It's on the job. We used to go out at night or during the day and put, you know, like, lay the wires from observation points to the headquarters, you know, from different-- from the first company to the second, you know. And we had also Morse code. And we had walkie talkies. And I was learning as I went along. We also had-- we had one and the-- of a-- of a American dodge. It's a covered, you know, and it was like a-- a radio station right there. Everything was there. And his name, I remember today, he was Jewish, his name was Deutsch. He was Jewish. He was-- he was the driver, and he was the operator there. And he had another two guys in there. And that-- that was like the communication that-- from-- for the headquarters. And from there, they dispersed like the small ones, you know, from the first, here, there, like in the fields, observation points. And the Germans used to cut them. We used to go out at night and fix these in the winter time. And to splice the wires was murder, murder. They used to shoot at us, the snipers, but then we used to shoot back. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] No, it-- it was a hard time. Until like we took Warsaw, like Lublin, took Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went-- first you were-- while-- how long before you got to-- to Lublin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lublin? Lublin we-- as soon as we landed in Kiwerce, we stayed there for a while. And then we took Lublin. And from Lublin went to [POLISH], you know, Warsaw. And then we-- as soon as we got to the German territory, by the Oder at Frankfurt, that was the hard battles. The Germans, were-- they had bunkers every 500 feet, those cement bunkers. And when-- we we got at those bunkers, there was-- was that high of ammunition to [INAUDIBLE]. There was no ammunition left already. They was-- they-- they didn't retreat. On the German soil, they were fighting to the death. And by the Oder, when we crossed the Oder, I remember--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your battalion was where?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the Oder, when we crossed the Oder at Frankfurt.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you-- but you weren't on-- on Warsaw side. You were--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. That was by the Oder already. Warsaw wasn't too bad. We stayed. One year, we stayed in Prague. And I-- I think I know the reason why they didn't want to take Warsaw. They could have taken Warsaw one year before. We were stationed in Prague, like in Palava, Prague, for one year. I was there already in communications, so I knew what was going on. And there was-- there was already the-- the Polish Army, called Armia Krajowa. They were in Warsaw. And they asked us we should throw down arms to them. But there was no way we could do, because the Germans were there. They would catch everything. And another thing, what I think personally--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- who was this-- I'm sorry, who was this that asked you to throw your-- was--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were the Polish underground army. They were communicating with London, was Mikolichek's government in London, England. And the Russians didn't want to take Warsaw. They wanted, I think, that everything should be wiped out. And when they go in, they would take like the communist government, had nothing to do with England, like the pre-war Polish Army, they're noncommunist. That's what I think, because they could have taken Warsaw a year before, and a lot of lives would have been saved, a lot of lives. When we came into Warsaw that night, everything was burning. We couldn't even cross the street. Everything was in rubble. When we got into the bunkers like on the other side, the Germans-- the-- the-- the stoves were still hot. From there, I think we went to Bydgoszcz, [POLISH] and--

speaker: interviewer
text: You're going-- slow down. You were in-- you were in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were--

speaker: interviewee
text: One night. Like, we went-- we went, we came, we crossed, I think it was about 1 o'clock at night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your group?

speaker: interviewee
text: My group. Well, the-- my division, like. And in the morning, the first thing in the morning, about 8, 9 o'clock, 10 o'clock in the morning, we started moving out again. And there was one well. It was called-- in Warsaw, there was a citadel. It was an underground there. The Germans used to-- it was Polish actually, but the Germans used to stay there. We were right near them when I saw a horse lying right by the well. And, you know, the-- the soldiers wanted water. As soon as I saw that horse, I said right away that the well is poisoned. And nobody took any water there. It was poisoned. They poisoned everything. And the whole city was burning, rubble. You-- you couldn't cross a street. There was no way to cross. You have to cross over the rubbles. No-- no jeep, no truck. No, the only one that could cross were horses.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, this was all done by the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: All by the Germans. No, no, it was like-- yeah, the Germans did all that, because when we were stationed in Prague, the Germans used to bomb Warsaw day and night every day. We used to watch the Russian MIGs, but there was no MIGs at that time. It was the [RUSSIAN], the Yaks and the German Messerschmitts. They used to fight over Warsaw every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they didn't-- they didn't bomb Prague where you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. They-- they-- they shot a few of the V-1's already. Remember, we heard about a V-2 and V-1 when they were bombing England. I saw a couple of them like-- like flying into Prague at the time, like the Germans did, like the rockets.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were attacking Warsaw, but not the battalion that you were in, the division.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Warsaw was-- Warsaw was under German occupation at the time. We were in Prague. Prague was occupied by the already the Russian-Polish Army. We were stationed at-- at that time at the [POLISH] Palava market in Prague.

speaker: interviewee
text: Which was how far from Warsaw? Just across the Wisla. That's all. I used to watch them change the guards on the-- on the Wisla. I was already, you know, at the observation point with binoculars, but we couldn't do nothing. We were-- we were just sitting and waiting until we got the orders from-- from the high commend. The high command was the Russia-- Russians, was Roborovski, [RUSSIAN]. Until-- until-- until we came to the Oder, and then it started again. The-- because from Russia, the Germans were running, running. Approximately when was this? What-- the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: '43, '44. When-- when they turned from Stalingrad, when I guess probably they capitulated, they-- they-- they couldn't get any supplies into Russia. Partisans used to blow up every-- every train, every-- they couldn't get by air, so they used to go by trucks and wagons. They took everything. There was-- there was no way they could get back into Russia. It was too far, so they didn't have enough supplies. They couldn't fight. But as soon as we came to-- to the Oder, that's when they really were fighting. A lot of lives, when we crossed the Oder, the fields were littered with bodies like flies, the Polish Army, the Russian Army, both. Anyway, after the Oder, already, it went fast.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long-- how long were you fighting in that battle? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, from-- from Warsaw till-- till we met with the American by the Elbe. I think-- I think April. April or May, the beginning of May. When I met with the Americans, that was it, finished. The war was finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- do you want to talk about what went on in battle with yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the battle, I was-- I was a soldier like everybody else. The only thing, I wasn't like-- I wasn't in the-- in the-- in the front lines before the Germans-- before the-- the-- the troops came. I wasn't there, like most of it, in the observation points, like ahead of the Army. I was always ahead of the Army. My-- my-- my company, always ahead. We used to be like most of the time on observation points, like, we used to go into a village, a house right between the Germans and us. And we used to send messages, like the artillery is here, the snipers is here, radios. Radios, they had already-- get mixed, like it's not radars, they had like to pinpoint these things. Because on the German soil already, we were like in the communications. I was sitting by the radio. Like, we used to take turns, you know, and-- and I soon as I left, another he was-- he was a Ukrainian boy. He was about maybe two years older than me. And he was sitting by the radio, by the window a little bit bigger than this, you know. And as soon-- as soon as I took off my earphones, walked away like into the hall, I heard a-- a shell. We knew already whether it's going to land here, or it's going to cross. We knew already. As soon as I heard that shell, I dropped my face down on the hall. That shell went right through the radio. His head was cut off. The-- the end of the-- was-- everything was smashed to pieces. Another, I don't know, 2 or 3 seconds maybe, it would have been me. Actually, I was lucky in the army. I wasn't touched. Mind you, I was like shell-- shell shock, because they used to throw bombs and grenades. They had cases of the grenades, they used to throw at us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it's like shrapnel. It's devastating. So a bomb fell not too far from me. I would say maybe the other side of the hall, like the earth and everything, you know, from-- from the-- I couldn't hear for a little while, but I was OK. I got out of it. Your division, approximately how many were left when-- when you finally met the Americans? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: My division was a-- division's got three, like, it's called a polk, battalions. And they used to-- they used to regroup every time we used to get killed. I couldn't tell you exactly. But we used to regroup every-- we used to be in the front line, let's say for two, three months. Then they used to give us a couple of weeks off, like in the back. Most of them were training the recruits.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when-- when you were off, where would you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to stay, like, in-- in towns, like in small towns. And just relax, just relax. But they were training new recruits at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was that one of your-- your jobs too?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that wasn't-- that was not my job, but the-- the officers' the companies'.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then when you met the Americans--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we met the Americans. It was at the Elbe. It was very-- it was very funny. Like, we were on this side of the Elbe, and the Americans were on the other side. In the middle, there were big, big German boats with people. They tried to escape. I used to-- I used to get on those boats because they were still on the-- on the-- on the-- on the side, where the-- where the Russian and Polish Army was. So I used to go in them. Then, when-- when we saw the Americans, we took a little boat, a row boat. There was-- two, yeah, two officers, me, and we went across in the boat, you know, to the Americans. We were-- you know, I had-- I had my rifle and everything. They had guns, because you couldn't leave any place without your guns. Anyway, we-- we shook hands and grabbed. And you know, they couldn't speak Polish or Russian. We couldn't speak English. So anyway, finally one Jewish guy and then one of the officers who was also Jewish, anyway we started to talk, like--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what language were you talking at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not me. They Were talking Jewish. Anyway, the other two stayed. I went back, because I figured if I go back, maybe I'll find somebody from the family. So they stayed on the American side already.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that you went on the boats where the people were trying to escape. These-- these weren't the army, the German Army?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they were German population, just--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was the population.

speaker: interviewee
text: --women, kids, you know, they-- like you see when the Russians are coming, the Germans were running. Like the German people, they were running very fast with their belongings, you know, with wagons and bicycles and whatever. Nobody-- nobody wanted to get into the Russian hands. Listen, what they did to the Russians, I don't blame them. And there was a lot of-- a lot of Russian, like, Jewish officers in the Russian Army, the Polish Army. And most of the doctors were Jewish, officers were Jewish. And it-- it wasn't so easy. See, under-- under Stalin, there was no such a thing as discrimination. If you would say dirty Jew, he'd send you to Siberia. You couldn't say nothing in the-- even the army. There was no discrimination whatsoever. Everybody was equal. There was no religion. But in the Polish Army, it was a little different. They-- they didn't like the Jewish officers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did you notice that, or did you hear things?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. No, no, no. I-- I-- I was in the middle of it. But they didn't know I was Jewish, so I heard every little thing. But I couldn't say nothing. But they didn't like them at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that with reason, or without reason? Or just because they were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just because they were Jews. They didn't like to take orders from Jews, from Jewish officers. There was quite a few Jewish officers, because every time they used to ask for volunteers, we used to go. Like, before any offensive, they send out to bring back [SPEAKING RUSSIAN]. [SPEAKING RUSSIAN] means in Russian, it's a tank. You had to bring back live soldiers. And whenever we used to go out to get them, one or two, we used to go out three, four, one or two came back. It wasn't easy.

speaker: interviewee
text: And every Jew was a volunteer, including me. Every time they wanted volunteers, I used to pick up my hand. Even the commander says don't you think it's enough? Every time? I said, no.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Jewish boys, the same thing. Every time they went first. They used to give us [SPEAKING RUSSIAN]. That's a ration, it's 100 gram of vodka before we went out.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that-- that's about it in the army. Then-- after the army, like in '45, we went back. We were stationed on the Czech-Polish border for a while. And then in 1946-- So you stayed there? You stayed with the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was still in the army, 1946. We landed. We came to Chenstochov. We were in barracks there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I took a bicycle and went into town. I take a look in town. [SPEAKING RUSSIAN], full of Jews. So I said, what the hell am I doing in the army? So I tried to get out. They wouldn't-- they wanted to send me to an officer's school. I didn't want to.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, I got out of the-- I-- I got out of the-- I-- I got-- we, like-- in Chenstochov, we moved. I was still in the army. I filled out my papers and everything. I got out, and I moved to, it's called [POLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you-- before you move out of the army, you-- you-- you said that you received several medals.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I have one-- one medal. It's called the Virtuti. This is for-- because of my deeds, what I did in the army. And the other medals was for like the campaigns that you go through, like the Oder and this in Baltic, and for liberating Warsaw, and the German-- the land there was-- was taken from Poland many years ago. So I got three medals and one Order. But one-- one of them is for bravery, whatever you call it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was from the Polish Army itself?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was from the Polish Army. I could have-- I could have gotten one, like, from the partisans, but I-- I didn't want to bother with it, like from the Russian partisans. I-- I could have applied for it, but what-- what do I need it for? What good is it going to do me?

speaker: interviewer
text: And finally, you got out of the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got out of the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the-- what was the date that you--

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1946, I got out of the army in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Summer, winter, fall?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a good question. I have it all in the-- in the-- in the-- in the papers there. Yeah, in-- in 1946, in-- in [POLISH]. That's when I got my Army discharge papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll continue after your discharge on the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
